opp.name: TXST_esourcing+risk+contract_April26
opp.ais_why:
• Customer Kevin at Texas State states a strong need around AI-led efficiency, compliance automation, and better UX versus SAP and manual work. Explicit AI mandate from the decision maker.
• Opportunity covers eSourcing, risk, and contract, with the description tagging AI-led efficiency as the primary differentiator versus the incumbent SAP/manual workflow.
• Created late April 2026 and still in early exploration, with next step to share Zycus decks and materials. Fresh AI-led pitch in flight, no Avoma calls yet to confirm depth.

items.quote: Kevin Kolb is Assistant Director, compliance/oversight role; no mention of who controls procurement platform budget or who would approve Zycus purchase. 'That'll go out for solicitation' (re: SAP RFP) implies formal process but no decision authority named.

Kevin stated TXST is part of Texas State University System (5-6 universities); unclear if procurement platform decisions are campus-level or system-level, who chairs evaluation, what RFP timeline/structure is.
Kevin described SAP pain, Jaggaer sync gap, manual compliance burden qualitatively; no cost, cycle-time, FTE impact, or savings target quantified.

items.area: Contracting, legal, InfoSec, IT integration process
items.quote: No discussion of MSA/SOW, legal review, InfoSec gate, IT stakeholder, or ERP integration scoping (critical for SAP BP1 or future replacement).
Zero contact roles logged; Kevin Kolb is the sole contact. No IT, Finance, senior Procurement, or system-level stakeholder identified or engaged.

items.area: Active evaluation or near-term compelling event
items.quote: Kevin stated call was informational/learning; SAP replacement RFP is 3-5 years out; no confirmation TXST is evaluating Jaggaer alternatives now. 'Maybe someday down the future you guys could fit into that.'

sources: Avoma discovery call 24 April 2026, Kevin Kolb (is_rep: false)
metrics.narrative: No quantified business case or ROI articulated by the buyer. Kevin Kolb described operational pain qualitatively: SAP BP1 is antiquated, requires manual p-codes, limited to 5 screens, poor user experience ('reminds me of Apple IIe programming'), and contract-spend data does not sync from SAP to Jaggaer, resulting in inaccurate budget reports ('making decisions based on information that's not even accurate'). Kevin also noted manual compliance checks for Texas SB20 and boycott screenings (8 of 11 checks automated via PaymentWorks, 3 remain manual). However, Kevin framed these as frustrations, not a quantified cost/impact (no cycle-time, error rate, FTE burden, or savings target stated). The call was explicitly informational ('I don't know what I'm going to get out of this... just trying to learn'), not a value-case build. Without a quantified pain or ROI, there is no compelling event to drive urgency or validate the $100k amount. Next move: re-engage Kevin to quantify the cost of manual workarounds, SAP inefficiency, and Jaggaer data-integrity gaps, and tie to a business case for near-term action.

sources: Avoma discovery call 24 April 2026, Kevin Kolb engagement and role
champion.narrative: No champion identified. Kevin Kolb is the sole engaged contact; he is an operational user (Assistant Director, compliance/oversight role) who took an informational/learning call. Kevin has no confirmed access to the Economic Buyer or decision-making authority. He demonstrated openness and curiosity ('I've been aware of Zycus for quite some time... this is the first time I've gone through and looked at your system... good to go through all that') and expressed intent to share Zycus info internally ('those are questions I will now ask... may I ask the same thing about Jaggaer'), but he has not advocated for Zycus, driven internal urgency, or introduced other stakeholders. Kevin's closing remark ('maybe someday down the future you guys could fit into that') signals passive interest, not active championing. No evidence of multi-threading (zero contact roles logged). Status: developing at best, more accurately gap—Kevin is an engaged contact, not a champion. Next move: assess whether Kevin can/will champion; if not, identify and engage a senior procurement or IT leader with budget authority and urgency to solve the stated pains.

sources: Avoma discovery call 24 April 2026, Kevin Kolb (is_rep: false)
competition.narrative: Jaggaer is the incumbent and the competitive baseline. Kevin stated TXST uses Jaggaer for marketplace (punch-outs, catalogs, contracts, solicitations/RFPs) and SAP BP1 for ERP; Jaggaer's interface is 'more user friendly' than SAP, and TXST plans to consolidate contract-spend from SAP into Jaggaer and replace SAP via RFP in 3-5 years. Kevin did not state TXST is evaluating alternatives to Jaggaer; rather, he framed Jaggaer as the long-term platform and SAP as the problem. When Bailey walked through Zycus (source, contract, procurement, invoice, risk), Kevin acknowledged the platforms 'parallel each other very well' and that Jaggaer is a Zycus competitor, but he expressed satisfaction with Jaggaer overall ('I may ask the same thing just like I did about SAP' suggests he has not previously questioned Jaggaer). No other competitor named. Competitive position: Zycus is the challenger to an entrenched, functional incumbent; buyer has not initiated a bake-off. Wedge: AI/Merlin differentiation (autonomous negotiation agents, contract AI, supplier risk automation) and the SB20/compliance pain (Jaggaer + PaymentWorks still require 3 manual checks; Zycus could automate all). However, Kevin has not validated these as compelling enough to displace Jaggaer. Next move: position Zycus as the AI-native alternative, quantify the ROI vs Jaggaer + PaymentWorks, and secure a formal benchmark/bake-off if TXST opens a sourcing process.

sources: Avoma discovery call 24 April 2026, Kevin Kolb (is_rep: false)
identify_pain.narrative: Pain confirmed but not quantified or owned by a senior sponsor. Kevin Kolb articulated specific operational pain: (1) SAP BP1 is outdated, requires manual p-codes, limited screen real-estate, poor UX ('reminds me of Apple IIe... back in BASIC'), (2) contract-spend data does not sync from SAP to Jaggaer, causing inaccurate budget reporting ('I'm making decisions based on information that's not even accurate... that's a frustration to me'), (3) manual compliance work—Texas SB20, boycott, country-of-operation checks—8 of 11 automated via PaymentWorks, 3 remain manual ('if the Zycus system could do it for us, that would be a plus... takes away from our manually having to go out and do the other 3 things'). Kevin also expressed personal/professional motivation: prevent stagnation, adopt AI tools, improve team efficiency. However, Kevin framed these as individual frustrations and learning goals, not organizational pain with executive sponsorship or budget allocated to solve them. He explicitly stated the call was informational, not driven by an active procurement need. Pain exists but is not yet tied to a compelling event, quantified cost, or senior owner. Next move: validate whether these pains are prioritized by TXST leadership, quantify the cost/impact, and identify the executive sponsor who would fund a solution.

sources: Avoma discovery call 24 April 2026, Kevin Kolb compliance references
paper_process.narrative: Paper process (contracting, legal, procurement, InfoSec, implementation) entirely unknown. Kevin did not discuss MSA/SOW requirements, legal review, IT security approval, or implementation/change-management process for onboarding a new procurement platform. As a Texas state university, TXST is governed by the relevant stakeholder rules and Texas University System policies (Kevin mentioned compliance obligations), implying formal procurement/contracting rigor, but specifics were not covered. No InfoSec, IT, or legal stakeholder identified. No reference to partner/SI involvement or ERP integration scoping (critical given Zycus would need to integrate with the relevant stakeholder1 or a future replacement). Status: gap. Next move: map the paper process—legal/contracting owner, IT/InfoSec gate, implementation/change-management lead—and understand lead times for each.

sources: Avoma discovery call 24 April 2026, Kevin Kolb role and scope
economic_buyer.narrative: Economic Buyer not identified. Kevin Kolb is Assistant Director of Procurement & Reporting, a compliance/oversight role; he stated his function is requisition approvals, P-card exceptions, contract validation, and state-mandated reporting—operational, not commercial or budget authority. Kevin made no reference to who controls procurement system budget, who would approve a Zycus purchase, or who owns the planned SAP replacement RFP ('that'll go out for solicitation'). He mentioned TXST is part of the Texas State University System with 5-6 universities, implying procurement decisions may be system-level, not campus-level, but did not name the decision authority. No CFO, CIO, VP Procurement, or system-level sponsor surfaced. The single contact (Kevin) is a user/evaluator, not a buyer. No EB contact role exists. Until the EB/budget owner is mapped, forecast and close-date are speculative. Next move: ask Kevin directly who owns the budget for procurement platforms and who would sponsor a Jaggaer alternative evaluation.

sources: Avoma discovery call 24 April 2026, Kevin Kolb org structure reference
decision_process.narrative: Decision process unknown. Kevin stated that TXST is part of the Texas State University System (5-6 universities) and that the planned SAP replacement will go out for RFP, implying a formal procurement process exists, but he did not describe it. He did not name approval layers (department, campus, system-level), RFP timeline, evaluation committee structure, or whether procurement platform decisions are made campus-level or system-level. Kevin's role is compliance/oversight, not decision-making, and he did not reference who leads sourcing decisions. No IT, Finance, or executive stakeholders mentioned. The single contact is insufficient to map the process. The Dec 2026 close date is not tied to any buyer timeline. Status: gap. Next move: ask Kevin to describe the approval chain for a procurement platform purchase, who chairs the evaluation, whether it is campus or system-driven, and what the realistic timeline is for a Jaggaer alternative evaluation (if any).

sources: Avoma discovery call 24 April 2026, Kevin Kolb pain points and interest areas
decision_criteria.narrative: Partial criteria inferred from Kevin's pain points, but no formal evaluation framework, scorecard, or RFP requirements discussed. Kevin highlighted desired capabilities: (1) user-friendly interface (contrasted SAP's p-code antiquity with Jaggaer's 'more intuitive' marketplace), (2) integrated contract-spend tracking (SAP-to-Jaggaer sync gap is a key pain), (3) automated compliance/risk checks (Texas SB20, boycott screenings, country-of-operation rules—wants all checks in one platform to eliminate manual work), (4) AI-driven efficiency ('trying to learn AI... don't want to be stagnant'). Kevin expressed interest in Zycus's autonomous negotiation agents and supplier risk module as potential fits. However, these are inferred preferences from a discovery conversation, not buyer-stated decision criteria or weighted scorecard factors. Kevin has not confirmed TXST is running an active evaluation, and he noted the SAP replacement RFP is 3-5 years out. No confirmation that a Jaggaer alternative is being benchmarked now. Status: partial (inferred fit topics exist, formal criteria unknown). Next move: validate whether TXST is actively evaluating alternatives to Jaggaer or SAP, and if so, co-develop decision criteria with Kevin and the system-level stakeholders.

ai_fit_signal.summary: Kevin Kolb is AI Curious: he attended a Zycus AI webinar, uses ChatGPT and Copilot daily for document creation and efficiency, and stated 'AI is the future, I'm trying to develop and not be stagnant.' He expressed skepticism about 'goofy and ridiculous' generative AI and concern about over-reliance ('I don't want AI to become my brain'), but he actively seeks AI tools to accelerate work and improve team efficiency. He engaged positively with Bailey's Merlin demo (autonomous negotiation, contract AI, supplier risk) and saw fit for TXST's compliance pain. However, he has not championed AI adoption organizationally or tied it to a budget/business case. AIS fields are null (no prior AI engagement score). Call evidence suggests Kevin personally is AI Curious; TXST organizationally is unknown (no IT/exec stakeholder engaged to validate institutional appetite). Tier: AI Curious (individual contact, not org-validated).

deal_movement.summary: Single discovery call 24 April 2026 (63 days ago); no progression since. NextStep (Bailey share Zycus decks) not confirmed delivered or acknowledged. Zero follow-up engagement. Deal is dormant.

items.name: Kevin Kolb
items.risk: Single point of contact; operational/compliance role, not decision authority; no evidence of championing or driving internal urgency; 63-day silence post-call suggests passive interest. Risk: deal anchored to one mid-level contact with no EB access will stall indefinitely.
items.title: Assistant Director of Procurement & Reporting
items.sentiment: Positive, curious, open to learning; expressed long-term awareness of Zycus and appreciation for the platform walk-through; however, framed engagement as informational/developmental, not driven by active need.

items.detail: No buyer-validated timeline or near-term compelling event. Kevin stated SAP replacement RFP is 3-5 years out; no confirmation of active Jaggaer evaluation. Dec 2026 close date is a placeholder, not anchored to buyer process. Call was informational ('maybe someday down the future'), not driven by urgency. Without a catalyst, deal will remain dormant indefinitely.

No executive or decision-authority stakeholder identified or engaged. Kevin Kolb is operational (Assistant Director, compliance/oversight), not EB/DM. No IT, Finance, VP Procurement, or Texas State University System lead surfaced. Zero contact roles logged. Without exec alignment and budget authority, deal cannot progress past informational stage.

Jaggaer incumbent entrenchment: Kevin stated TXST is consolidating into Jaggaer (moving contract-spend from SAP to Jaggaer) and expressed overall satisfaction ('I may be pretty happy with that platform'). Jaggaer is perceived as the long-term solution; Zycus is a known name but has not displaced Jaggaer in buyer's mind. No active bake-off or dissatisfaction driver. Inertia/status-quo bias is high.

No budget discussion, no Economic Buyer identified, no approval process mapped. $100k amount is speculative. Kevin did not reference allocated funds, procurement platform budget, or who controls it. Without validated budget and EB, forecast is indefensible.

Kevin noted TXST has ~5-6 universities in Texas State University System and procurement decisions may be system-level. Change-management complexity (multi-campus rollout, system-level approval, user training across universities) not scoped. If procurement platform decisions are system-driven, sales cycle and stakeholder map expand significantly; if campus-driven, Kevin's influence is limited. Org structure and change process unclear.

63-day silence post-discovery call: NextStep (Bailey share Zycus decks) not confirmed delivered or acknowledged. No follow-up by buyer or seller. Risk: Kevin's interest was exploratory/polite, not committed; deal may be dead and not yet disqualified. Must re-engage within 7 days to validate continued interest or close as lost.

His closing remark ('maybe someday down the future') signals passive interest, not active championing. Zero contact roles logged; no evidence Kevin can or will mobilize the EB or decision process. Deal is single-threaded to a non-champion.

items.action: Re-engage Kevin Kolb directly to validate continued interest and qualify or disqualify within 7 days.
items.trigger: 63 days of silence since single discovery call; NextStep (share Zycus decks) not confirmed delivered or acknowledged; Kevin's last words 'maybe someday down the future' signal passive interest; zero contact roles, no EB/DM/champion, no validated timeline/budget.
items.expected_effect: Determine if Kevin received materials, if TXST has near-term interest in evaluating Jaggaer alternatives, and if he can/will introduce EB/senior Procurement lead. If no, disqualify and move to long-term nurture. If yes, execute rank-2 and rank-3 moves immediately. Clears pipeline of speculative forecast or reactivates a stalled deal.

items.action: Quantify the cost of the status quo and build a written business case anchoring Zycus ROI to TXST pain points.
items.trigger: Kevin articulated qualitative pain (SAP antiquated, Jaggaer sync gap causes inaccurate budget decisions, 3 manual compliance checks) but no quantified cost, FTE burden, cycle-time impact, or savings target; no compelling event to drive urgency; Jaggaer entrenched.
items.expected_effect: Convert Kevin's frustrations into a quantified business case (e.g. X FTE hours/year on manual SB20 checks, $Y budget error risk from SAP-Jaggaer sync gap, Z% cycle-time reduction via Merlin autonomous negotiation). Arm Kevin (or future champion) with a data-driven case to justify Zycus evaluation internally and create urgency vs Jaggaer status quo. Moves deal from informational to qualified stage.

items.action: Request introduction to senior Procurement Director, CIO, or Texas State University System procurement lead who owns platform budget and decisions.
items.trigger: Kevin Kolb is Assistant Director (compliance/oversight, not decision authority); zero contact roles; no EB/DM identified; Kevin stated TXST is part of Texas State University System (5-6 universities), implying procurement platform decisions may be system-level; single-threaded deal will stall.
items.expected_effect: Multi-thread to decision-authority stakeholder(s). Validate whether procurement platform decisions are campus-level or system-level, who controls budget, and whether there is executive appetite for a Jaggaer alternative evaluation. Without EB/senior sponsor, deal cannot progress to qualified stage and Dec 2026 close is a fiction. If Kevin cannot/will not introduce, disqualify.

items.action: Position Zycus AI/Merlin wedge in a written competitive comparison vs Jaggaer and secure a formal benchmark or pilot.
items.trigger: Jaggaer incumbent, functional, expanding; Kevin satisfied overall; no active bake-off; Zycus AI differentiation (Merlin, autonomous negotiation, contract AI, supplier risk automation) demoed but not tied to quantified ROI or competitive displacement strategy.
items.expected_effect: Create a written AI/ROI narrative: Zycus automates all 11 compliance checks (eliminates PaymentWorks + 3 manual steps), fixes SAP-Jaggaer contract-spend sync gap via unified data model, and delivers negotiation/cycle-time savings Jaggaer cannot match (less AI-native per Bailey). Propose a time-boxed benchmark or pilot (e.g. run one RFP cycle in Zycus vs Jaggaer, compare cycle-time and savings). Shifts deal from 'nice to know' to active competitive evaluation, raising win probability and creating urgency.

items.action: Identify and leverage a Texas higher-ed reference customer to build peer credibility and de-risk TXST decision.
items.trigger: Kevin asked 'are you part of any other universities in Texas already?' (none confirmed); he is 18-year procurement veteran, iSEM member, familiar with peer benchmarking; TXST is part of Texas State University System (5-6 universities), implying peer influence matters; no reference customer offered on 24 April call.
items.expected_effect: Provide Kevin (and future EB/senior stakeholders) with a named Texas higher-ed or peer public-university reference customer (ideally one who replaced Jaggaer or SAP with Zycus). Arrange a reference call to validate Zycus AI ROI, implementation experience, and compliance fit (Texas SB20, public procurement rigor). Peer validation de-risks the decision and counters Jaggaer entrenchment, raising TXST's confidence in Zycus as a viable alternative.

Time-in-Qualified unknown but exceeds 60 days with zero demonstrated progression. Close date Dec 2026 is a placeholder, not a buyer-validated timeline.

north_star_verdict.headline: 63 days dark after single exploratory call with no buying motion. Kevin stated no immediate need, consolidating into Jaggaer, SAP RFP 3-5 years out. No champion, no next step executed, zero progression. Disqualify or hard reset required.

flags: No validated opportunity: call was informational/learning ('I don't know what I'm going to get out of this... maybe someday'), no budget/timeline/EB/process confirmed, Dec 2026 close is a placeholder. Qualify or disqualify within 14 days—validate whether TXST has budget/urgency for a Jaggaer alternative now or whether this is a 3-5 year nurture (in which case, move to long-term pipeline and stop forecasting). A qualified deal has a champion with access to the EB, a quantified business case, and a validated decision process; this has none.
best_practice_check.summary: This deal is dark and likely unqualified: single exploratory call 63 days ago, no follow-up, zero contact roles, no EB/DM/champion, no validated timeline or budget, and incumbent (Jaggaer) is entrenched with no active displacement effort. Critical actions: (1) re-engage Kevin to confirm interest and deliver promised materials, (2) qualify or disqualify against MEDDPICC bar—map EB, validate timeline/budget, secure multi-thread—within 14 days, (3) if qualified, build urgency by quantifying status-quo cost and positioning Zycus AI wedge in a formal benchmark.

competitive_position.summary: Jaggaer is the entrenched incumbent, functional and growing (TXST consolidating into it). No active evaluation or bake-off. Zycus is a known name (Kevin aware for years via webinars) but has not displaced Jaggaer in Kevin's mind. Threat level: Jaggaer high (status quo, no pain severe enough to disrupt); SAP BP1 medium (replacement RFP in 3-5 years, not immediate); do-nothing high (no urgency, call was exploratory). Wedge: AI/Merlin, autonomous negotiation, compliance automation (SB20/boycott checks), contract-spend data integrity. However, wedge not yet activated with buyer. Single current threat: inertia/Jaggaer entrenchment.

competitors.name: Jaggaer
competitors.quote: We use Jaggaer for our marketplace... the interface is more user friendly... we're trying to move everything into Jaggaer... I may be overall pretty happy with that platform... parallel each other very well... they're one of your biggest competitors.
competitors.source: Avoma discovery call 24 April 2026, Kevin Kolb
competitors.how_we_win: Position Zycus AI/Merlin differentiation (autonomous negotiation agents, contract AI redlining, supplier risk automation including Texas SB20/boycott checks) as the next-generation upgrade over Jaggaer's less-AI-native platform. Quantify ROI: eliminate 3 manual compliance checks, fix SAP-Jaggaer contract-spend sync gap, accelerate sourcing cycle-time. Secure a formal benchmark if TXST opens evaluation.

competitors.name: SAP BP1
competitors.quote: SAP... reminds me of back in the nineties... using p-codes... you can only have up to 5 screens... it's so old school... antiquated... SAP does not send contract-spend information back to Jaggaer, so budget reports are inaccurate... we're looking to replace SAP in the next 3 to 5 years via RFP.
competitors.source: Avoma discovery call 24 April 2026, Kevin Kolb
competitors.how_we_win: SAP replacement is a 3-5 year horizon, not immediate. Zycus is not an ERP competitor (Kevin confirmed understanding). However, Zycus can integrate with SAP or its replacement and solve the contract-spend sync pain Jaggaer cannot. Use SAP pain as proof-point for need to consolidate procurement functions into a modern, AI-native platform (Zycus) rather than layering Jaggaer + PaymentWorks + manual workarounds.

competitors.name: PaymentWorks
competitors.quote: We use PaymentWorks for supplier vetting... it does 8 of 11 checks; we manually do the other 3 things that aren't already being done.
competitors.source: Avoma discovery call 24 April 2026, Kevin Kolb
competitors.how_we_win: PaymentWorks is a point solution for supplier onboarding/vetting, not a competitive procurement platform. Zycus Supplier Risk module can replace PaymentWorks and automate all 11 compliance checks (Texas SB20, boycott, country-of-operation) in one platform, eliminating manual work and integration complexity. Position as consolidation play.

competitors.name: Do Nothing / Jaggaer entrenchment
competitors.quote: Maybe someday down the future you guys could fit into that... I don't know what I'm going to get out of this call, just informational, trying to learn.
competitors.source: Avoma discovery call 24 April 2026, Kevin Kolb
competitors.how_we_win: Create urgency by quantifying the cost of the status quo: manual compliance burden (FTE hours, risk exposure), SAP-Jaggaer data-integrity cost (bad decisions, budget overruns), and Jaggaer's AI gap (cycle-time, negotiation savings left on table). Tie to a near-term compelling event (e.g. upcoming contract renewal, a system-level procurement transformation initiative, or a peer university win). Without urgency, this deal remains exploratory indefinitely.

items.source: Avoma discovery call 24 April 2026, Bailey commitment to Kevin Kolb

Kevin: 'Thank you for your introduction... continue to work on my understanding of what's going on here so that maybe someday you guys could fit into that.'

ai_positioning_strength.score: Partial (foundation laid, not yet leveraged as wedge)
ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI positioning was well-received but not activated. Kevin is AI-curious ('AI is the future, trying not to be stagnant'), uses ChatGPT/Copilot daily, and attended a Zycus AI webinar (what prompted the call). Bailey demoed Merlin, autonomous negotiation agents, contract AI, and supplier risk automation; Kevin acknowledged the capabilities and expressed interest ('that's interesting... that would be a plus'). However, Kevin did not connect Zycus AI to a quantified business case, urgent need, or internal advocacy. AI was positioned as a learning topic, not a competitive wedge or ROI driver. Score: partial—foundation laid, not yet leveraged. Under-positioned: yes—Merlin/AI differentiation vs Jaggaer was not tied to TXST's SAP pain, compliance burden, or a compelling event to drive action. Next move: re-engage with a quantified AI ROI story (e.g. autonomous negotiation savings, compliance automation FTE hours saved, contract AI cycle-time reduction) and position Zycus as the AI-native alternative Jaggaer cannot match.

items.quote: I know AI is the future... I'm trying to develop and not be stagnant... I use ChatGPT and Copilot to help take my thoughts and put them into what looks presentable much more... I want to learn the tools and help my team become better.
items.source: Avoma discovery call 24 April 2026, Kevin Kolb

items.quote: We manually do 3 checks that aren't already being done by PaymentWorks... if the Zycus system could do it for us, that would be a plus... takes away from our manually having to go out and do the other 3 things.
items.source: Avoma discovery call 24 April 2026, Kevin Kolb
items.criterion: Automated compliance and supplier risk (Texas SB20, boycott checks)

items.quote: SAP interface... is so old school, reminds me of Apple IIe... Jaggaer's marketplace is more user friendly, easier to see things, easier to run reports.
items.source: Avoma discovery call 24 April 2026, Kevin Kolb

items.quote: SAP does not send contract-spend information back to Jaggaer, so when you look at budget for that contract it shows zero spend... I'm making decisions based on information that's not even accurate... that's a frustration to me.
items.source: Avoma discovery call 24 April 2026, Kevin Kolb
items.criterion: Contract-spend data integrity and reporting accuracy

items.quote: I don't know what I'm going to get out of this call, to be honest... just informational, trying to learn... maybe someday down the future you guys could fit into that.
items.source: Avoma discovery call 24 April 2026, Kevin Kolb
items.criterion: Immediate procurement need or active evaluation

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Partial fit confirmed on AI adoption, compliance automation, and user experience; gaps on timeline urgency and stakeholder engagement. Kevin is AI-curious and manually burdened by compliance; Zycus capabilities align. However, Kevin's expectation was learning/informational, not evaluation, and he has not validated an active need or timeline.

hard.ais_why:
• Customer Kevin at Texas State states a strong need around AI-led efficiency, compliance automation, and better UX versus SAP and manual work. Explicit AI mandate from the decision maker.
• Opportunity covers eSourcing, risk, and contract, with the description tagging AI-led efficiency as the primary differentiator versus the incumbent SAP/manual workflow.
• Created late April 2026 and still in early exploration, with next step to share Zycus decks and materials. Fresh AI-led pitch in flight, no Avoma calls yet to confirm depth.

pulse.summary: Live: buyer call(s) read this sweep.

rep_outreach.note: rep reached out, awaiting buyer reply

A deal anchored to one mid-level contact with no decision authority will stall or ghost.

deltas.subject: Kevin noted TXST has ~5-6 universities in Texas State University System and procurement decisions may be system-level. packets.subject: Zycus:Send Zycus platform slide deck and short overview materials to Kevin Kolb for future benchmarking

value.name: Kevin Kolb
value.risk: Single point of contact; operational/compliance role, not decision authority; no evidence of championing or driving internal urgency; 63-day silence post-call suggests passive interest. Risk: deal anchored to one mid-level contact with no EB access will stall indefinitely.
value.title: Assistant Director of Procurement & Reporting
value.source: Avoma discovery call 24 April 2026, txstate.edu email domain match
value.sentiment: Positive, curious, open to learning; expressed long-term awareness of Zycus and appreciation for the platform walk-through; however, framed engagement as informational/developmental, not driven by active need.

value.name: Jaggaer
value.quote: We use Jaggaer for our marketplace... the interface is more user friendly... we're trying to move everything into Jaggaer... I may be overall pretty happy with that platform... parallel each other very well... they're one of your biggest competitors.
value.source: Avoma discovery call 24 April 2026, Kevin Kolb
value.how_we_win: Position Zycus AI/Merlin differentiation (autonomous negotiation agents, contract AI redlining, supplier risk automation including Texas SB20/boycott checks) as the next-generation upgrade over Jaggaer's less-AI-native platform. Quantify ROI: eliminate 3 manual compliance checks, fix SAP-Jaggaer contract-spend sync gap, accelerate sourcing cycle-time. Secure a formal benchmark if TXST opens evaluation.

value.name: SAP BP1
value.quote: SAP... reminds me of back in the nineties... using p-codes... you can only have up to 5 screens... it's so old school... antiquated... SAP does not send contract-spend information back to Jaggaer, so budget reports are inaccurate... we're looking to replace SAP in the next 3 to 5 years via RFP.
value.source: Avoma discovery call 24 April 2026, Kevin Kolb
value.how_we_win: SAP replacement is a 3-5 year horizon, not immediate. Zycus is not an ERP competitor (Kevin confirmed understanding). However, Zycus can integrate with SAP or its replacement and solve the contract-spend sync pain Jaggaer cannot. Use SAP pain as proof-point for need to consolidate procurement functions into a modern, AI-native platform (Zycus) rather than layering Jaggaer + PaymentWorks + manual workarounds.

value.name: PaymentWorks
value.quote: We use PaymentWorks for supplier vetting... it does 8 of 11 checks; we manually do the other 3 things that aren't already being done.
value.source: Avoma discovery call 24 April 2026, Kevin Kolb
value.how_we_win: PaymentWorks is a point solution for supplier onboarding/vetting, not a competitive procurement platform. Zycus Supplier Risk module can replace PaymentWorks and automate all 11 compliance checks (Texas SB20, boycott, country-of-operation) in one platform, eliminating manual work and integration complexity. Position as consolidation play.

value.name: Do Nothing / Jaggaer entrenchment
value.quote: Maybe someday down the future you guys could fit into that... I don't know what I'm going to get out of this call, just informational, trying to learn.
value.source: Avoma discovery call 24 April 2026, Kevin Kolb
value.how_we_win: Create urgency by quantifying the cost of the status quo: manual compliance burden (FTE hours, risk exposure), SAP-Jaggaer data-integrity cost (bad decisions, budget overruns), and Jaggaer's AI gap (cycle-time, negotiation savings left on table). Tie to a near-term compelling event (e.g. upcoming contract renewal, a system-level procurement transformation initiative, or a peer university win). Without urgency, this deal remains exploratory indefinitely.

value.detail: No buyer-validated timeline or near-term compelling event. Kevin stated SAP replacement RFP is 3-5 years out; no confirmation of active Jaggaer evaluation. Dec 2026 close date is a placeholder, not anchored to buyer process. Call was informational ('maybe someday down the future'), not driven by urgency. Without a catalyst, deal will remain dormant indefinitely.
